other_observers: Randy Siebert, Glenn Walbek, Gregg Goodrich, Loch Kilpatrick, Norm Erthal, Jim and Bill Schmoker, Henry Armknecht; I was the first to identify the birds in flight. I do not know who spotted them on the ground as our group was split up at the time.
specific_location: I believe it was on the east side of CR QQ just south of CR 4. I'm using Mapquest to generate this info so I may not be looking at an accurate map, but I think that's where it was. The fields in question are private property owned by the brother of Henry Armknecht, who was with us and gave us permission to enter.
description: The birds we saw on the ground were pipit-shaped, with medium-length, thin bills (not sparrow bills), pale legs, and medium-length tails, a little shorter than American Pipit but close to that length. The birds were a pale sandy brown above and whitish below. They were densely streaked with darker brown on the crown, nape, and (I think) the sides of the breast. They didn't have bright white edgings to any back or wing feathers, though the buffy edgings to those feathers did create a faint pair of buffy wingbars as well as a complex, rather scaled pattern above. The birds' tails were dark, with white outer tail feathers visible when they flew. The faces of the birds were rather plain, without a lot of streaking on the auriculars, throat, lores or supercilium, and the black eyes looked quite large.Behaviors: At least one of the birds flushed out of 6-8 inch grass on the side of a ridge, then was seen flying high to the east. This or a different bird, and a second individual, were relocated by the other half of our group on the ground in a field planted to winter wheat. The birds were walking and foraging on the ground. We observed them for about ten minutes through the scope, until they flew off together, calling. While we were watching the two birds on the ground, a third bird flew over us giving the "squeet" call. We heard other birds giving this call later, but were not able to see them or determine whether they were different from the three birds we had encountered earlier.Call: The birds gave the "squeet!" call, which was how we located them and identified them in flight. One bird was never seen; it flew over, calling, as we were watching two others on the ground.  The call of the Sprague's Pipits we were hearing was a single-syllable, high-pitched squeak with rising intonation, often repeated. It was loud and emphatic.Plumage: presumed adult
